metadata.summary: Production patterns for Claude API: model selection, prompt caching, Batch API, token counting, backoff, and cost monitoring in multi-agent pipelines.

content.title: Claude Best Practices
content.summary: A set of production patterns for calling the Anthropic Messages API reliably and cost-efficiently: model tier selection (Haiku/Sonnet/Opus by task), prompt caching for repeated large contexts, Batch API for offline workloads, token counting for pre-flight validation, exponential backoff for rate limits, and a monitored client wrapper for cost attribution per subagent role.

why.p: The Anthropic API has several non-obvious behaviors that silently degrade production systems: max_tokens defaults are low and truncate structured output mid-JSON; prompt cache requires byte-identical prefixes (any dynamic injection before the cached block busts the cache); model aliases update without notice; rate limit errors include a retry-after header that should be parsed rather than guessed. Applying these patterns prevents silent failures in multi-agent pipelines.

list.item: Building any production system that calls the Anthropic Messages API.
Optimizing cost/quality tradeoff across multi-agent pipelines.
When system prompts are large and repeated across many requests (prompt caching).
Implementing retry logic, fallback models, or monitoring for Claude API calls.
Selecting the right Claude model tier for a given agent role.
Quick scripted one-off calls with no cost or reliability concerns.
Provider-neutral code that must abstract over multiple LLM providers — use an abstraction layer instead.
Token counting pre-flight adds latency; skip for simple short prompts.

section.name: model-tier-selection
section.p: Model capability and cost scale together. Using the wrong tier either wastes budget or produces degraded output.

rule.statement: Use Haiku for formatting, routing, classification, and simple extraction tasks. Use Sonnet for generation, code, and analysis. Use Opus for complex reasoning, architecture, and strategy tasks.
rule.rationale: Using Opus for a simple routing decision wastes ~10x the budget. Using Haiku for complex reasoning produces lower-quality output that may require re-work.

rule.statement: Pin exact model IDs in production configs (e.g., "claude-sonnet-4-20250514") rather than aliases ("claude-sonnet-4").
rule.rationale: Aliases can update to a newer model version without notice. A silent model upgrade can change output format or behavior, breaking downstream parsing.

rule.statement: Log the model actually used in every response, not just the model requested. Log this when implementing fallback logic.
rule.rationale: Fallback to a cheaper model is invisible to the orchestrator unless explicitly logged. Downstream agents may produce worse outputs without knowing the model was downgraded.

example.title: Model tier selection with explicit logging
example.code:
import anthropic
MODELS = { "router": "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022", "generator": "claude-sonnet-4-20250514", "reasoner": "claude-opus-4-5-20251101", }
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
def call(role: str, messages: list, max_tokens: int = 2048) -> str: model = MODELS[role] r = client.messages.create( model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens, messages=messages ) if r.stop_reason == "max_tokens": raise ValueError(f"Response truncated for role={role}. Retry with higher max_tokens.") return r.content[0].text
example.note: max_tokens is explicit and stop_reason is checked before returning the response.

section.name: token-configuration
section.p: Explicit token management prevents silent truncation of structured output.

rule.statement: Always set max_tokens explicitly on every Messages API call.
rule.rationale: The default max_tokens is low and silently truncates structured output mid-JSON, producing invalid payloads that are hard to debug because the API returns HTTP 200.

rule.statement: When stop_reason == "max_tokens" on a structured output call, the JSON is truncated and invalid. Retry with higher max_tokens; do not parse the response.
rule.rationale: A truncated JSON response from a structured output call will raise a parse error downstream. The correct action is to retry with more headroom, not to handle the parse error.

section.name: prompt-caching-structure
section.p: Prompt caching reduces cost by 90% on cache hits but requires careful prefix management.

rule.statement: Place the most stable content at the top of the system prompt with cache_control: ephemeral. Put dynamic instructions last.
rule.rationale: Cache uses prefix matching. Dynamic content at the top busts the cache on every call. Stable content at the top maximizes the proportion of input tokens that are cached.

rule.statement: Never inject timestamps, request IDs, or user-specific values before the cached block.
rule.rationale: Even a single character difference in the prefix causes a cache miss. You pay the write cost (25% above regular) with no read benefit.

rule.statement: Measure cache_read_input_tokens / input_tokens ratio per pipeline run. Target above 0.70.
rule.rationale: Below 70% cache hit rate, write overhead starts eroding the 90% read savings. Low hit rate indicates the cached prefix is being modified per call.

example.title: Cached system prompt structure
example.code:
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic()
# Cached system prompt structure STATIC_DOCS = "... large stable documentation (10k+ tokens) ..."
system = [ { "type": "text", "text": STATIC_DOCS, "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"} # cached prefix }, { "type": "text", "text": dynamic_instructions # not cached; appended per-run } ]
response = client.messages.create( model="claude-sonnet-4-20250514", max_tokens=4096, system=system, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_task}] )
# Monitor cache effectiveness u = response.usage hit_rate = u.cache_read_input_tokens / max(u.input_tokens, 1)
example.note: Static docs are marked cached; dynamic instructions appended without cache_control.

section.name: batch-api
section.p: The Batch API provides 50% cost savings for workloads that tolerate latency.

rule.statement: Use Batch API for workloads that tolerate up to 24-hour latency to receive 50% cost reduction.
rule.rationale: Batch API cuts cost in half with no other code changes. The only requirement is that the workload is not real-time: offline enrichment, nightly analysis, evaluation suites.

section.name: token-counting
section.p: Pre-flight token counting prevents costly failures on large inputs.

rule.statement: Use client.messages.count_tokens() for pre-flight checks before paying for large-input calls.
rule.rationale: Token counting is cheap (no generation cost) and prevents paying full generation cost for inputs that exceed the context limit, which fail with an API error.

section.name: retry-and-rate-limits
section.p: Intelligent retry logic maximizes throughput without overwhelming the API.

rule.statement: Parse the retry-after header from RateLimitError instead of guessing the backoff interval.
rule.rationale: The Anthropic API includes a retry-after header with the exact wait time. Guessing with exponential backoff may wait too long or too short, degrading throughput unnecessarily.

rule.statement: Implement exponential backoff with jitter for 5xx server errors; retry up to 3 times before raising.
rule.rationale: 5xx errors are transient. A single retry captures most transient failures. More than 3 retries risks cascading delays in multi-step pipelines.

rule.statement: In multi-worker orchestrators, use a shared rate-limit token bucket rather than per-process backoff.
rule.rationale: Per-process backoff does not account for other workers consuming quota simultaneously. A shared bucket prevents all workers from hammering the API at the same time after a backoff period.

section.name: model-fallback
section.p: Silent fallback creates difficult-to-debug quality variations.

rule.statement: When falling back from Sonnet to Haiku on rate limiting, log the actual model used in the response.
rule.rationale: The orchestrator needs to know which model produced a given output. Silent fallback produces unexpected quality variations that are impossible to audit without this log.

section.name: async-processing
section.p: Asynchronous concurrency is the correct pattern for parallel API calls.

rule.statement: Use AsyncAnthropic with asyncio.gather for concurrent batch calls. Do not spawn threads; the SDK is async-native.
rule.rationale: The Anthropic Python SDK is built on httpx with native async support. Using threads adds overhead and can introduce race conditions. asyncio.gather is the correct concurrency primitive.

example.title: Monitored client with async support
example.code:
import anthropic import logging import time
log = logging.getLogger(__name__) client = anthropic.Anthropic()
def call(model: str, system: list, messages: list, max_tokens: int = 2048): t0 = time.monotonic() r = client.messages.create( model=model, max_tokens=max_tokens, system=system, messages=messages ) elapsed = time.monotonic() - t0 total_tok = r.usage.input_tokens + r.usage.output_tokens log.info("model=%s tokens=%d elapsed=%.2fs", model, total_tok, elapsed) return r
example.note: Actual model ID is logged (not the requested alias), enabling post-hoc audit of fallback behavior.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: The orchestrator constructs a reusable system prompt containing static context (docs, instructions) and marks it cache_control: ephemeral. Each subagent call reuses this cached prefix, appending only the per-turn user message. Costs for repeated large-context calls drop ~90% on cache hits. A thin monitoring wrapper tracks token usage and cost per subagent role; the orchestrator uses this to select the cheapest model capable of the task (Haiku for formatting/routing, Sonnet for generation, Opus for complex reasoning).
section.note: Recommended subagents: claude-router (selects model tier based on task complexity signal), cost-monitor (aggregates token usage per pipeline run; alerts when cost per run exceeds threshold), retry-wrapper (implements exponential backoff for RateLimitError and 5xx; falls back to Haiku on sustained Sonnet rate limiting).

section.name: tool-definition-best-practice
section.p: Tool descriptions must answer "when should I call this?" rather than "what does it do?"

example.title: When-to-call tool description
example.note: The description answers the LLM's question "should I call this tool?" with actionable guidance.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: Common failure modes when integrating Claude into production agent systems.

antipattern.description: Cache hit requires identical prefix — any runtime injection (timestamps, request IDs) placed before the cached block breaks the cache silently.
antipattern.reason: The cache mechanism uses byte-for-byte prefix matching. Any character difference, even whitespace, causes a cache miss. Dynamic content must always come after the cached block.

antipattern.description: RateLimitError from Anthropic includes a retry-after header, but guessing the backoff interval instead of parsing it.
antipattern.reason: The API provides the exact wait time. Guessing may be too conservative (wasting throughput) or too aggressive (getting rate-limited again).

antipattern.description: Fallback to Haiku on rate limiting is invisible to the orchestrator; downstream agents may produce worse outputs without knowing why.
antipattern.reason: The orchestrator cannot adjust for quality degradation if it doesn't know which model actually ran. Always log the model used.

antipattern.description: Tool schemas with additionalProperties: false reject unexpected keys; LLMs sometimes add extra fields — strip unknown keys before passing tool results back.
antipattern.reason: The API validates against the schema. Invalid keys cause tool use to fail. Strip them before returning the result to the model.

antipattern.description: Extended thinking (thinking param) blocks count toward output tokens but are non-cacheable; large think budgets can 5-10x the cost of a single call.
antipattern.reason: Thinking tokens bypass the cache and are expensive. Budget them carefully for truly complex reasoning tasks only.

antipattern.description: Human-in-loop checkpoint: when stop_reason == "max_tokens" on a structured output call, the JSON is truncated and invalid — do not parse it; retry with higher max_tokens.
antipattern.reason: Truncated JSON will fail parsing. The correct action is to retry with more headroom, not to handle the parse error.

template.purpose: System prompt with static cached prefix and dynamic tail.
Minimal Claude client with token usage and latency logging.
